description: This figure presents a comprehensive multiscale characterization of the initial microstructure of a hot-rolled Fe-15.5Mn-3.95Al-5Ti-2.15B (wt.%) low-density steel, focusing on TiB₂ particle distributions and matrix phases. Panel (a) displays a pseudo-3D SEM image showing primary (coarse, polygonal) and eutectic (fine, lamellar) TiB₂ particles distributed across RD, TD, and ND surfaces. High-magnification SEM in (b) reveals hexagonal prism-shaped primary TiB₂ particles on the RD surface, while (c) distinguishes the dual-phase matrix via Mn-rich austenite and Al-rich δ-ferrite regions with corresponding EDS mapping. XRD pattern (d) confirms three phases: δ-ferrite (bcc), austenite (fcc), and hexagonal TiB₂ with indexed peaks. Micro-CT reconstructions in (e) illustrate the 3D spatial distribution of particles across size domains from 0.8–5.0 μm to 20.0–42.7 μm. Statistical histograms (f) and (g) reveal a critical disparity: small particles (0.8–5 μm) constitute ~45% of the number fraction but only ~1% of the volume fraction, whereas coarse particles (10–42.7 μm) occupy >80% of the volume despite lower number density. This bimodal distribution confirms that coarse primary TiB₂ particles, inherited from solidification and retained after hot rolling, dominate the microstructural volume fraction within the ferrite-austenite dual-phase matrix.
